meta.title: 2.4 Rendering Lists
meta.description: When you see .map() in React, it is turning an array of data into a list of components.

blocks.prompt: Think about a list of search results, a feed of social media posts, or a table of customer records. Each item looks the same but has different data. Before reading this module, write down your guess: if you have an array of 50 products, how does React turn that into 50 product cards without writing the same component tag 50 times?

blocks.answer: It renders 2 list items: 'Kim' and 'Sam'. The .filter() step removes Pat because active is false. Only Kim (active: true) and Sam (active: true) pass through the filter. Then .map() turns each remaining user into an <li> with their name. Pat does not appear at all.

options: A) An unordered list with three items: Red, Green, Blue
B) The text 'Red, Green, Blue' as a single paragraph
C) Three separate unordered lists, one for each color
problems.explanation: .map() transforms each string in the array into an <li> element. The three <li> elements are placed inside a single <ul>, creating one unordered list with three items. The key prop uses the color name itself (acceptable here since the values are unique strings).

options: A) Nothing is wrong -- this code works correctly
B) Using index as the key is fragile -- if the list is reordered, added to, or items are removed, React may mix up which component belongs to which product
C) The .map() function cannot receive a second parameter
problems.explanation: Using the array index as a key is a common mistake. If a product is deleted or the list is sorted, the indexes shift and React associates the wrong data with the wrong DOM elements. The fix is to use a unique identifier like a product ID. However, these products do not have IDs -- this is also a data modeling issue. You might tell a developer: 'These products need unique IDs, and the key should use those IDs instead of the array index.'

options: A) Tell the AI: 'Each mapped element is missing a key prop. Add key={notif.id} to the outer div in the .map() callback.'
B) Tell the AI: 'The conditional className is wrong -- swap read and unread.'
C) Tell the AI: 'The .map() should be a .forEach() instead.'
problems.explanation: The mapped elements are missing the key prop. Every element returned from .map() in React must have a unique key. The fix: <div key={notif.id} className={...}>. The conditional className logic is correct (read notifications get the 'read' class). .forEach() would not work here because it does not return values -- .map() is the correct method for transforming data into JSX.

blocks.prompt: Explain the .map() pattern in React in your own words. Why does every mapped element need a key prop? What goes wrong if you use the array index as the key?

blocks.content: .map() transforms an array of data into an array of components -- one item in, one component out. Every mapped element must have a unique, stable key prop so React can track items correctly. Using array indexes as keys causes bugs when the list changes. Missing keys are the most common AI mistake in list rendering code.

blocks.prompt: You have now learned the complete Level 2 toolkit: props for passing data, conditional rendering for showing and hiding, and .map() for rendering lists. The checkpoint will test all of these skills together with realistic multi-component scenarios.

reviewQuestions.question: What does .map() do when used inside JSX?
options: It filters out unwanted items from an array
It transforms each item in an array into a component or element
It sorts the array in alphabetical order
reviewQuestions.explanation: .map() goes through each item in an array and transforms it into a piece of JSX -- one item in, one component out.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: That's it! .map() is the bread and butter of list rendering in React.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: Not quite -- .map() transforms each array item into a component. Think of it as a machine: data goes in, JSX comes out, one piece at a time.

reviewQuestions.question: Why should you avoid using the array index as the key prop in .map()?
options: React does not accept numbers as keys
Indexes shift when items are added, removed, or reordered, causing React to mix up which component belongs to which data
Using index makes the list render slower
reviewQuestions.explanation: Array indexes change when the list changes. If you delete item 2, item 3 becomes index 2, and React thinks it's the same item -- leading to display bugs.
reviewQuestions.encourageCorrect: Great understanding! Using stable, unique IDs as keys keeps React from getting confused when lists change.
reviewQuestions.encourageIncorrect: The issue is that indexes shift when items change. If you remove an item, all the indexes after it change, and React associates the wrong data with the wrong elements. Use unique IDs instead.
